title: After forced shutdown, the image tag will be lost.
body:
### Issue Description

After executing the `podman pull` command, forcefully shut down the system (simplified understanding as pulling the power plug). Upon restarting, the image tag will be set as NONE.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman pull ubuntu
2. Force shutdown VM
3. Launch VM
4. Image tag is NONE


### Describe the results you received

```
<none>                   <none>      e4c58958181a  4 weeks ago   80.4 MB
```

### Describe the results you expected

```
docker.io/library/ubuntu                   latest      e4c58958181a  4 weeks ago   80.4 MB
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

apple hv(vfkit)

### Additional information

When I run `podman pull ubuntu` again, the tag will be reverted back, and it won’t re-pull (since the `layers` are still there), causing the tag to be lost.

comments.body:
The situation seems to be like this: When I run `podman pull`, the tag data is not immediately written to a certain location.

However, when I perform a normal shutdown operation and start again, the tag is not lost.

The issue is the kernel has not synced the tagging to disk yet, so "pulling the plug" looses the data. Not sure there is much we can do about this. I suppose we could force a system sync at the moment we write the tag, but this does not guarantee writing.

@vrothberg @giuseppe @nalind WDYT?

BTW. Apart from the data in `/var/lib/containers`, all other data will be cleared after a reboot.

![39-02 18 39@2x](https://github.com/containers/podman/assets/8198408/96fffd22-0098-4cfc-aa94-13d9d90f8c6a)

> The issue is the kernel has not synced the tagging to disk yet

@rhatdan Does the kernel here refer to the Linux kernel? If so, what are the relevant kernel parameters?
You don't want to fiddle with that.  is this an on going problem or just something you witnessed once? IE is your machine always being forced off right after image pulls?

> You don't want to fiddle with that. is this an on going problem or just something you witnessed once? IE is your machine always being forced off right after image pulls?

In our scenario, we will first shut down the vm normally, but 200ms later, we will forcibly shut down the vm, to avoid the vm process from persisting. So, it becomes a matter of timing. If the Linux kernel can write to the hard drive within 200ms, there will be no problem. However, if it fails to do so, the current issue will arise.

I can also adjust the 200ms to 1 second, but I think this would only lower the frequency of the problem. If possible, I would like to truly solve this issue.

I’m not quite sure why only tags have this issue, while other data doesn’t have this problem. From your previous explanation, it seems like tags exist in memory, and before shutting down, the sync mechanism of the Linux kernel ensures that data in memory is written to the hard drive.The solution I can think of now is to periodically write the tags and hashes to a certain file. When restarting, if the Tag of a certain image is found to be *NONE*, read the file and automatically set it to the previous tag.
do you use `sync()` before shutting down the VM?

@giuseppe I gave it a try, everything worked fine after executing the `sync` command after `podman pull`. Maybe we could do as @rhatdan  suggested, “I suppose we could force a system sync at the moment we write the tag, but this does not guarantee writing.”
how are initiating the force shutdown ?

@baude I’m using `vfkit`. After starting the virtual machine, execute `podman pull`. Press <kbd>CTRL + C</kbd> to force quit and reproduce this issue.

If you need to reproduce the test locally, you can let me know if your computer is `arm64` or `amd64`, and I will provide the corresponding *kernel/initrd/rootfs* files, as well as the commands for vfkit and gvproxy.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
